fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small text utility library — basically something that cleans up indented blocks of text that developers embed inside already-indented code. The core idea is: when you write a multi-line string inside a function or class, all those lines pick up the surrounding indentation, and we want to strip that shared prefix out automatically so the output is clean and left-aligned. Relative indentation between lines should stay intact though — if one line is deeper than another, it should stay that way after processing.

We also need this to work on raw binary buffers, not just decoded strings, similar to how we handled the binary path in that old encoding module. And there's a Windows line-ending thing — if the content uses CRLF, those carriage returns need to survive the transformation.

A few edge cases I know about: blank lines and whitespace-only lines should be handled gracefully (they shouldn't blow things up or count toward the indent calculation). Also there's something about the first line being special — I think if the block starts immediately on the first line vs. starting after a line break changes the behavior slightly, but I'm fuzzy on the exact rule there.

Need a CLI adapter for testing, plus an automated test harness that runs JSON-based test cases. Keep the core logic separate from the I/O stuff please.

One detail to lock down: the shared indent is figured out from only the lines after the first line. On top of that, lines that are entirely empty or contain only whitespace characters are excluded from that calculation entirely. The minimum is taken across all remaining lines; if no such lines exist, zero characters are removed. Relative indentation still stays intact the same way described before.

Also, the first line is never unindented. If content shares the opening line, meaning there is no leading line break before the first word, that content is emitted verbatim with no leading whitespace removed. The only time the first-line behavior changes is when the text begins with a line-feed character (\n). In that case, that leading empty line is dropped entirely from the output, and unindentation begins from what was the second line. Put another way, when the input text begins with a line-feed character (\n), the empty line that would appear as the first line of output is dropped. The effective first line of output becomes what was the second line of the input. This is the mechanism that lets developers start a block on its own line without getting a blank first line in the result.

One more whitespace edge case: if a line contains only whitespace characters (spaces and/or tabs) and that whitespace run is shorter than the computed common indentation amount, emit it as a completely empty line in the output. Its whitespace is consumed entirely rather than leaving a partial prefix behind. And for line endings, a trailing line-feed character in the input produces a trailing empty line in the output, effectively a final newline. The transformation does not strip or add terminal line breaks beyond what the indentation removal requires.

For the binary side, Feature 2 (unindent_bytes) should use the exact same line-splitting and indentation-removal behavior, just at the raw byte level using byte values for line-feed (0x0A), space (0x20), and tab (0x09). The input there comes in as a sequence of integers 0–255 passed as JSON array under key 'data' with mode 'bytes'.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When operating on a raw byte buffer, the identical indentation-removal logic is applied at the byte level. Lines are delimited by the line-feed byte (0x0A), space bytes (0x20) and tab bytes (0x09) count as one unit each for indent measurement, and the input does not need to be valid decodable text. The output is the transformed byte sequence. The CLI adapter receives byte values as a JSON array of integers in the range 0–255 under the key 'data', and the mode selector is 'bytes'.

When input lines are terminated by carriage-return + line-feed pairs (\r\n), the transformation splits on the line-feed character only. A carriage-return that immediately precedes the very first line-feed (i.e., part of a leading \r\n) is not treated as content — the leading empty line is still dropped. All other carriage-return characters that terminate retained lines are preserved verbatim in the output, so the CRLF line-ending style of the input survives the unindentation unchanged.

trigger_keywords: how does the test adapter receive input
JSON command structure for the adapter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter (CLI entry point) reads a single JSON object from stdin. For text mode the object contains 'mode': 'text' and 'text': <string>. For bytes mode it contains 'mode': 'bytes' and 'data': <array of integers 0–255>. The adapter writes only the raw transformed payload to stdout — no metadata, no PASS/FAIL markers. For bytes mode the output is the raw bytes themselves written to stdout.

trigger_keywords: how to run the test suite
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The automated test harness is invoked via 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. It accepts an optional '--cases-dir <subdir>' argument (default: 'test_cases') pointing to a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/. For each case it writes one file to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt containing only the raw stdout from the program under test. Output is namespaced by cases-dir so different runs never overwrite each other.
A single line of text containing no line-feed characters is returned unchanged. The empty string is also returned unchanged. Neither case has any indentation to compute or remove.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the first line being special — I think if the block starts immediately on the first line vs. starting after a line break changes the behavior slightly
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Rule: (a) if text begins with \n, drop that leading empty line and unindent all subsequent lines; (b) if content appears on line 0 with no preceding \n, the first line is emitted verbatim (never unindented) and the minimum indent is computed only from lines index 1 onward

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details about that aspect handy — could you check the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or look at how the core transformation handles that scenario? The general principle is: strip the smallest common leading whitespace from all non-blank lines after the first, drop a leading blank line if present, and preserve everything else including relative indentation and line endings.

feature1_unindent_text.json.description: Remove the common leading indentation from a block of multi-line text. The amount removed is the smallest leading run of spaces/tabs found across every line except the first, ignoring lines that are empty or contain only whitespace. If the text begins with a line break, that empty leading line is dropped. Content sharing the first line with no preceding break is left untouched, deeper-indented lines keep their extra indentation, blank lines stay blank, and whitespace-only lines shorter than the common indent collapse to empty. Inputs cover space indentation, tab indentation, content starting on the first line, interior blank lines with deeper nesting, whitespace-only short lines, a preserved trailing line break, a single line, and the empty input.

feature2_unindent_bytes.json.description: Apply the same common-indentation removal to a raw byte buffer rather than to text, returning the transformed bytes. The input is a sequence of byte values; the smallest leading run of space/tab bytes across all lines after the first is stripped from every line, an initial empty line (leading newline byte) is dropped, and the byte content is otherwise preserved. Inputs cover a space-indented buffer and a tab-indented buffer.

feature3_carriage_returns.json.description: Unindent text whose lines are terminated by a carriage-return + line-feed pair. Lines are split on the line-feed; a leading carriage-return that precedes the very first line break is not treated as part of the content. The common leading indentation is computed and removed exactly as for line-feed-only text, and the carriage-return characters that terminate the retained lines are preserved in the output so the CRLF line endings survive the transformation. Inputs cover tab-indented CRLF text and space-indented CRLF text with a trailing line break.
